context: He said, I'm the Lord, I change not.
point_summary: God's immutability — "I change not" — is invoked as the reason believers must conform their thinking to Scripture rather than expect God's Word to accommodate cultural preferences.

context: You know what God said in the book of Malachi? Prove me. Prove me.
point_summary: Giving an offering is an act of faith, illustrated by God's own challenge in Malachi 3:10 — "Prove me" — meaning those who claim they cannot afford to give are failing to trust God's promised blessing.

context: God said of Himself, I'm the same yesterday, today, and forever.
point_summary: God's immutability — "I am the same yesterday, today, and forever" — is invoked to insist that His capacity for both grace and judgment seen throughout Scripture reflects one consistent divine character.

context: You know what He said there in the book of Malachi? Prove me. He was speaking contextually about giving.
point_summary: Malachi 3:10's "prove me" invitation is cited as a paradigm for trusting God's faithfulness over self-reliance, with giving as the concrete test case where God promises both blessing and rebuking the devourer.

context: In Malachi, they were receiving rebuke because they were robbing God. How are we robbing God? In tithes and offerings.
point_summary: Robbing God in tithes and offerings (Mal. 3:8) illustrates that giving is not transactional but a recognition that everything already belongs to God.

context: He says that He changes not,
point_summary: God's immutability — "He changes not" — grounds the claim that decisions built on His unchanging Word will endure, unlike those built on shifting human opinion.

context: Notice what the Bible says in Malachi chapter one. In verse number six.
point_summary: God's own rhetorical question in Malachi 1:6 — "If I be a father, where is mine honor?" — grounds the call to orderly, reverent worship by pressing the logical obligation owed to God as Father and Master.

context: Malachi chapter one, verse number six. Last book of the Old Testament just before the Gospel of Matthew. Malachi chapter number one, verse number six. A son honoreth his father and a servant his master. If then I be a father, where is mine honor? If I be a master, where is my fear? Sayeth the Lord of hosts unto you, O priest that despise my name. And you say, wherein have we despised thy name?
point_summary: God's demand for honorable worship is underscored by His rebuke of Israel's polluted offerings—blind and lame animals given to Him that no earthly governor would accept.

context: Notice what he says, ye offer polluted bread upon mine altar. And ye say, wherein have we polluted thee? In that you say, the table of the Lord is contemptible.
point_summary: Offering defective sacrifices to God—blind or lame animals of no personal value—exposes a contemptuous heart that withholds the best from the Lord while reserving it for oneself.

context: Notice in verse eight, if you offer the blind for sacrifice, is it not evil? And if you offer the lame and sick, is it not evil?
point_summary: Bringing defective animals to God's altar—what would be rejected by a human governor—exposes the contempt of offering God anything less than one's best.

context: Notice what he says in verse number eight. He says, offer it now unto thy governor. Will he be pleased with thee? Or accept thy person, sayeth the Lord of hosts.
point_summary: Offering defective animals to God while withholding them from earthly governors exposes the hypocrisy of worship that honors men above the Lord.

context: Well, let me read verse 11. For from the rising of the sun, even under the going down of the same, that my name shall be great among the Gentiles.
point_summary: God's name will ultimately be honored among the Gentiles with pure offerings, contrasting the polluted, deficient sacrifices Israel was bringing to the altar.

context: I'm thankful as you look through and understand in verse 14, notice what God says.
point_summary: God's greatness as King is not diminished by Israel's corrupt offerings; his name remains dreadful among the heathen even when his own people bring him less than their best.

context: And every place in sin shall be offering to my name and a pure offering. For my name shall be great among the heathen, sayeth the Lord of hosts.
point_summary: God's name will one day be great among the Gentiles and receive pure offerings worldwide, contrasting sharply with Israel's corrupt, blemished sacrifices that dishonored his worthiness.

context: but cursed be the deceiver, which hath in his flock a male, and voweth and sacrifices unto the Lord a corrupt thing. Notice where he says, for I am a great king, sayeth the Lord of hosts, and my name is dreadful among the heathen.
point_summary: God's declaration "I am a great king" and his name being "dreadful among the heathen" grounds the sermon's insistence that he is worthy of our very best offering, not the corrupt or defective.

context: If you rob God, and we see that in Malachi
point_summary: Withholding the tithe is robbing God, and the consequence is that He will reclaim it through unexpected expenses, as illustrated by a personal testimony of a car repair costing exactly the unpaid tithe amount.

context: He said there in Malachi, I am the Lord, I change not.
point_summary: God's immutability—"I am the Lord, I change not" (Mal. 3:6)—grounds the sermon's assurance that the same truths Elisha taught his servant remain reliable and applicable for believers today.

context: I am the Lord, I change not.
point_summary: God's immutability—his unchanging nature declared in Malachi 3:6—is cited alongside Hebrews 13:8 to establish that Christ's eternal, unchangeable priesthood rests on the same divine constancy.

context: For I am the LORD, I change not; therefore ye sons of Jacob are not consumed.
point_summary: God's immutability, declared in Malachi 3:6 ("I am the Lord, I change not"), is the reason Israel was not consumed — and grounds present rejoicing that the God we serve remains unchanging.

context: And in verse number six of Malachi chapter number three, notice what God says. I am the Lord. I change not. I change not. Therefore, ye sons of Jacob are not consumed.
point_summary: God's declaration "I change not" in Malachi 3:6 grounds the doctrine of divine immutability, with the implication that Israel's survival depends precisely on God's unchanging character.

context: The Lord says in Malachi, I am the Lord, I change not.
point_summary: God's immutability — that He does not change — is cited from Malachi 3:6 as a specific reason for Thanksgiving-season rejoicing amid a world of constant, unsettling change.

context: Malachi says this, speaking of the attribute of God. He says in Malachi chapter number 3 and verse number 6, he says, I am the Lord, I change not. Therefore, ye sons of Jacob are not consumed.
point_summary: God's immutability—his never-changing nature—is presented as the doctrinal foundation that makes Scripture trustworthy and salvation consistent across every generation.

context: He says, I am the Lord, I change not. Therefore, ye sons of Jacob are not consumed.
point_summary: God's immutability — declared in "I am the LORD, I change not" — is the very reason the sons of Jacob were not consumed, grounding confidence in Scripture's plan of redemption and the consistency of salvation across all ages.

context: You say that was then. I'm the Lord, I change not.
point_summary: God's unchanging nature ("I am the LORD, I change not") grounds the confidence that He who delivered Hezekiah from Assyria remains equally able to act on behalf of His people today.

context: You know what God says in Malachi? He says, prove me, prove me, and see if I won't do this.
point_summary: God's own invitation in Malachi 3:10 to "prove me" is cited as evidence that He stands behind His word and can be trusted to do what He promised.

context: That God said that you've robbed me in the book of Malachi, where have we robbed thee? In tithes and offerings.
point_summary: Withholding the tithe constitutes robbing God, but faithful tithing is an act of faith that God honors with blessing and by rebuking the devourer on the giver's behalf.

context: And he said that prove me is what he said in Malachi chapter 3. And what would he do? Not only would he bless them, but he would also rebuke the devourer for their sakes.
point_summary: Tithing is framed as an act of faith—trusting that God will do more with ninety percent than a person could accomplish with one hundred, and that obedience unlocks His promised blessing and rebuking of the devourer.

context: even from the days of your fathers you're gone away from mine ordinances and have not kept them. Return unto me and I will return unto you, saith the Lord of hosts. But ye said, wherein shall we return?
point_summary: Israel's long history of departing from God's ordinances frames the call to return through faithful giving as an act of covenant obedience, not mere financial duty.

context: Will a man rob God? Yet ye have robbed me. But ye say, wherein have we robbed thee? God says in tithes and offerings.
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings is not merely financial negligence but robbery against God himself, making faithful giving an act of worship owed to him.

context: He says, you've robbed God. Ye say, wherein have we robbed thee? God says in tithes and offerings.
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings constitutes robbing God, a charge Malachi's audience was not merely accused of but found guilty of, bringing a curse upon the entire nation.

context: It says, you're accursed with a curse for you have robbed me. Even this whole nation.
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings constitutes robbing God, bringing a curse upon the guilty, with the remedy being faithful giving that invites divine blessing.

context: Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse, that there may be meat in mine house. Notice what he says, and prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it.
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings constitutes robbing God, bringing a curse, while faithful giving invites God's promised blessing of opened heavens—a remedy demanding proof through obedience.

context: wherein have we robbed thee in tithes and offerings?
point_summary: Robbing God in tithes and offerings brings a curse, but faithful giving opens the windows of heaven to blessing so abundant there is not room enough to receive it.

context: Bring ye all the tithes in the storehouse. He says, prove me in the passage, prove me now herewith, sayeth the Lord. If I will not open you the windows of heaven and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it.
point_summary: Faithful tithing is presented as the condition God himself invites his people to test, promising that obedience will open heaven's windows of blessing and rebuke the devourer—illustrated by the miraculous provision in 2 Kings 7.

context: And I will rebuke the devourer for your sakes, and he shall not destroy the fruits of your ground, neither shall your vine cast her fruit before the time in the field, saith the Lord of hosts.
point_summary: Faithful tithing carries the promise that God will rebuke the devourer and protect the harvest, illustrated by the miraculous abundance God provided Israel after famine in 2 Kings 7.

context: And all nations shall call you blessed, for you shall be a delightsome land, saith the Lord of hosts.
point_summary: Faithful tithing, God promises in Malachi 3:12, transforms a cursed, starving nation into a "delightsome land" blessed before all nations—illustrated here by Israel's sudden abundance after the Assyrian camp's spoil in 2 Kings 7.

context: the indictment against these people in Malachi 3
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings constitutes robbing God and brings a curse, while faithful giving invites divine blessing and protection from the devourer—opportunities available only in this present life.

context: God talks about, prove me in the book of Malachi, contextually with regard to giving, but he says, prove me.
point_summary: Malachi 3:10's "prove me" is invoked briefly to illustrate that God invites bold, expectant prayer about things only he can accomplish, though the verse's tithing context is acknowledged and set aside.

context: I am the Lord, I change not.
point_summary: God's immutability — that he does not change — is the ground for confidence that the same God who provided for the widow and for Hudson Taylor will provide today.

context: A son honoreth his father and a servant his master. If then I be a father, where is mine honor? And if I be a master, where is my fear? Sayeth the Lord of hosts unto you, O priest that despise my name. And you say, wherein have we despised thy name?
point_summary: Malachi 1:6–8 is invoked as a contrast to Abraham's example, showing that offering God anything less than one's best—blind, lame, or sick sacrifices—dishonors the Father and Master he is.

context: Ye offered polluted bread upon mine altar. And you say, wherein have we polluted thee? And that ye say, the table of the Lord is contemptible.
point_summary: Malachi 1:7's polluted offerings are contrasted with Abraham's lavish hospitality to show that Abraham, unlike Israel's priests, brought his very best before the Lord.

context: if ye offer the blind for sacrifice, is it not evil? And if you offer the lame and sick, is it not evil? Offer it now unto thy governor. Will he be pleased with thee? Or accept thy person? Sayeth the Lord of hosts.
point_summary: Offering blind, lame, and sick animals to God—sacrifices one would never dare present to a human governor—exposes the contempt underlying half-hearted worship, contrasted here with Abraham's deliberate, costly hospitality.

context: prove God, prove God. That's what he says in the book of Malachi. He says, prove me, prove me.
point_summary: Families are called to "prove God" in personal, lived experience so that children witness his faithfulness firsthand, not merely hear of it through Scripture.

context: In Malachi, God says prove me. Prove me!
point_summary: God's invitation in Malachi to "prove me" is cited as the biblical basis for bold, expectant prayer that genuinely tests God's ability rather than settling for timid faith.

context: In fact, in Malachi he says, you wouldn't even take it to your governor but yet you offer it to me
point_summary: Offering blemished sacrifices to God—what one would not dare present to a human governor—is condemned in Malachi as evidence that Israel withheld from God the preeminence He alone deserves.

context: I am the Lord, I change not.
point_summary: God's immutability—cited from Malachi 3:6 ("I am the Lord, I change not")—grounds the sureness of his covenant promises, contrasting his unchanging nature with humanity's constant fluctuation.

context: God said come back. You know in Malachi chapter 3 God says this, He said if you will I will.
point_summary: Malachi 3 is cited to establish a "you will, I will" principle of conditional restoration: God promises to receive back those who return to Him, but the initiative of coming home belongs to the wayward.

context: I am the Lord. I change not
point_summary: God's immutability — "I am the Lord, I change not" — is cited to establish that in Israel's spiritual wandering, it was the people who moved away, not God.

context: You see that in Malachi chapter three, God said, if you will, I will.
point_summary: Malachi 3 is cited as scriptural warrant for the conditional "if you will, I will" pattern—God's promised response to revival being contingent on His people moving first in humility and prayer.

context: I am the Lord, I change not, therefore you sons of Jacob are not consumed
point_summary: God's immutability—"I change not"—is cited from Malachi 3:6 as scriptural grounding for James's assertion that the Father of lights has "no variableness," assuring believers that the source of every good gift is eternally constant.

context: He said, I change not.
point_summary: God's immutability ("I change not") grounds the certainty that He can be known, accepted, or rejected, but never redefined by human confusion or false religion.

context: God doesn't say to these people in Malachi 3, I'm gonna give you new instruction.
point_summary: God's call in Malachi 3 is not renegotiation of his principles but a straightforward command to return, because he does not bargain or adjust his instruction to suit human circumstances.

context: Notice with me as we continue reading in verse number seven, and I will return unto you, saith the Lord of hosts, but ye said, wherein shall we return?
point_summary: God's call to "return" in Malachi 3:7 is presented as an unconditional command, not a negotiation—God does not revise his instruction to suit human circumstances but demands that his people amend their ways.

context: Then notice verse eight. Will a man rob God?
point_summary: Robbing God—not merely neglecting a duty—is the charge Malachi levels against those who withhold what God has commanded, and God's response is not renegotiation but a call to return unchanged terms.

context: will a man rob God? Yet ye have robbed me. But ye say, wherein have we robbed thee? In tithes and offerings.
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings constitutes robbing God, and financial hardship may be directly connected to that disobedience—a link the sermon presses by opening financial counseling conversations with the question of tithing faithfulness.

context: Ye are cursed with a curse, for ye have robbed me, even this whole nation.
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings constitutes robbing God, and financial hardship may be directly connected to that disobedience through the curse declared in this passage.

context: God says you're cursed with a curse. Why? Because you've robbed me. Robbed me how? With tithes and offerings.
point_summary: Withholding tithes and offerings constitutes robbing God and brings a curse, making financial hardship inseparable from one's faithfulness in giving.

context: God then says this to the nation of Israel in Malachi 3 and verse 10. Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse. Then notice that there may be meat in mine house and prove me now herewith, saith the Lord of hosts. If I will not open you the windows of heaven and pour you out a blessing, that there shall not be room enough to receive it, and I will rebuke the devourer for your sakes.
point_summary: Malachi 3:10's command to bring the full tithe is presented not as prosperity theology but as God's covenant promise to provide for needs when his people honor him faithfully.

context: And he shall not destroy the fruits of your ground, neither shall your vine cast her fruit before the time in the field, saith the Lord of hosts.
point_summary: God's promise in Malachi 3:11 to rebuke the devourer is presented not as prosperity preaching but as a principle of divine provision: faithful tithing invites God's care over needs, not guaranteed material wealth.

context: prove me now
point_summary: God's promise in Malachi 3:10–11 is not prosperity preaching but a covenant assurance that faithful giving invites God to meet needs and rebuke destruction, as the divine challenge "prove me now" makes clear.

context: if I will not open you the windows of heaven and pour you out a blessing that there shall not be room enough to receive it
point_summary: God's promise in Malachi 3:10 is cited as the basis for trusting that faithful giving will be met with divine provision of needs — not necessarily material abundance, but God's active care, even in practical, everyday ways.

context: and I will rebuke the devourer for your sakes. He shall not destroy the fruits of your ground, neither shall your vine cast her fruit before the time in the field, saith the Lord of hosts
point_summary: God's promise in Malachi 3:11 to "rebuke the devourer" means that faithful giving invites divine protection over one's material provision, illustrated as God supernaturally extending the life of ordinary possessions.

context: we talk about in Malachi, we read it earlier, tithes
point_summary: Robbing God in tithes and offerings (Mal. 3:8) is reframed through David's doxology: since all things come from God, withholding any portion is absurd, because we only return what was already His.

context: a son honoreth his father and a servant his master if then I be a father where is mine honor if I be a master where's my fear saith the Lord of hosts unto you Oh priest that despised my name and ye say wherein have we despised my name
point_summary: God's demand for genuine, prescribed worship is underscored by Israel's offering of blind, lame, and sick animals — outward religious acts that God rejected because they violated his stated requirements.

context: upon mine altar and you say wherein have we polluted polluted thing in that you say the table of the Lord is contemptible
point_summary: Offering blemished sacrifices—blind, lame, and sick animals unfit even for a governor—exposes Israel's worship as hollow ritual rather than genuine honor rendered to God.

context: if you offer the blind for sacrifice is it not evil and if you offer the lame and sick is it not evil offer it now unto thy governor
point_summary: Offering blind, lame, and sick animals instead of unblemished sacrifices—gifts no governor would accept—exposes Israel's ritual as contemptible performance rather than true worship.

context: And now I pray you beseech God that he will be gracious unto us This has been by your means and he regard your person sayeth the Lord of hosts
point_summary: Defective offerings brought to God — blind, lame, and sick animals the governor himself would reject — expose Israel's worship as hollow ritual rather than true worship.

context: Who is there even among you that would shut the doors for not neither do you kindle fire on mine altar for naught? I have no pleasure in you sayeth the Lord of hosts neither will I accept an offering at your hand
point_summary: God's rejection of Israel's defiled offerings in Malachi 1:10 illustrates that outward religious activity is not true worship when it withholds one's best from a God worthy of it.

context: from the rising of the Sun Even under the going down to the same my name shall be great among the Gentiles and in every place incense shall be offered unto my name
point_summary: God's declared greatness among the Gentiles in Malachi 1:11 stands as a sharp contrast to Israel's profaned, half-hearted offerings, exposing their ritual activity as something less than true worship.

context: but ye have profaned it in that you say the table of the Lord is polluted and the fruit thereof even as meat is Contemptible
point_summary: Offering defiled or inferior sacrifices to God—what the people called worship—is exposed here as no worship at all, because God as great King accepts nothing less than one's best.

context: you said also behold what a weariness it is it? And you have snuffed at it sayeth the Lord of hosts and you brought that which was torn and the lame and the sick
point_summary: Bringing defiled, substandard offerings while calling it worship is not true worship — God, as the great King worthy of our best, refuses to accept it.

context: Thus you brought an offering should I accept this at your hand of your hand sayeth the Lord?
point_summary: Offering blemished animals while vowing to give one's best exposes hollow worship — going through the motions without genuine reverence for God as the great King who deserves nothing less than true devotion.

context: for I am a great king sayeth the Lord of hosts and my name is dreadful among the heathen
point_summary: God's declaration "I am a great king" and that His name is "dreadful among the heathen" grounds the sermon's insistence that He is worthy of genuine worship, not corrupt, going-through-the-motions offerings.

context: a son honoreth his father and a servant his master. If then I be a father, where is mine honor? And if I be a master, where is my fear? Sayeth the Lord of hosts unto you, O priests that despise my name
point_summary: Malachi 1:6 is introduced to press the point that acknowledging God as Master (Adonai) demands not merely surrender but costly sacrifice, contrasted with Israel's contemptible offering of blind and lame animals.

context: We see this name Adonai in verse number 14
point_summary: Malachi 1:14 is introduced to illustrate that recognizing God as Adonai—Lord and Master—demands not only surrender but genuine sacrifice, contrasted with Israel's offering of defective animals.

context: and if you offer the blind for sacrifice, is it not evil? And if you offer the lame and sick, is it not evil?
point_summary: Offering God what one would be ashamed to present to a human governor exposes the contradiction of treating the Lord as less worthy than earthly authority, and therefore less deserving of one's best.

context: and now I pray you beseech God that he will be gracious unto us. This hath been by your means. Will he regard your person, sayeth the Lord of hosts
point_summary: God's rhetorical question in verse 9 — whether He will regard the persons of those who bring defiled offerings — exposes the absurdity of expecting divine favor while withholding the best from the One worthier than any human governor.

context: who is there even among you that would shut the doors for not? Neither do you kindle fire on mine altar for not. I have no pleasure in you, sayeth the Lord of hosts, neither will I accept an offering at your hand
point_summary: God's rejection of worthless offerings in Malachi 1:10 is underscored by the pointed challenge that what Israel would never dare present to a human governor, they were casually bringing before the Lord of hosts.

context: For from the rising of the sun, even unto the going down of the same, my name, my name shall be great among the Gentiles and in every place and shall be offered unto my name and a pure offering for my name shall be great among the heathen, sayeth the Lord of hosts
point_summary: Despite Israel's contemptible offerings, God declares in verse 11 that His name will be great among the Gentiles — a point underscored by the triple repetition of "my name" in that single verse.

context: but ye have profaned it. In that, you say, the table of the Lord is polluted and the fruit thereof, even his meat is contemptible
point_summary: Israel's profaning of God's name by calling his table polluted and his offerings contemptible stands in direct contrast to the thrice-repeated declaration in verse 11 that his name shall be great among the Gentiles.

context: Ye said also, behold, what a weariness it is, is it? And ye have snuffed at it, sayeth the Lord of hosts
point_summary: God's rebuke of Israel's contemptible, weary offerings in Malachi 1:13 is applied to the present-day call for believers to offer worthy living sacrifices of body and praise, not corrupt or halfhearted worship.

context: Cursed be the deceiver, which hath in his flock a male and voweth and sacrifices unto the Lord a corrupt thing. For I am a great king, sayeth the Lord of hosts, and my name is dreadful among the heathen
point_summary: God's self-declaration as "a great king" whose name is "dreadful among the heathen" grounds the sermon's call to offer worthy sacrifice, connecting ancient Israel's corrupt offerings to the believer's present duty of living and praise sacrifices.

context: Think about the truth of Malachi chapter number 1 and the sacrifice
point_summary: God's insistence that His name be great among the nations (v. 11) exposes the priests' polluted offerings as a direct affront to His majesty as a great King whose name is dreadful among the heathen.

context: to rebuke the devourer. We read that in Malachi and God says, prove me.
point_summary: Tithing is framed as an act of faith and obedience, trusting God's promise in Malachi to rebuke the devourer and stretch the remaining ninety percent beyond what the full amount could accomplish.

context: and we find you know the children of Israel guilty of that as well Malachi they were doing that they were bringing things oh this isn't any good for me so we'll offer it to the Lord
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: making application broader than what it's used in Malachi three
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He says, I am the Lord, I change not
point_summary: God's immutability — "I am the Lord, I change not" — grounds the assurance that what God did in Acts 13–14 He can still do today.

context: God loved Jacob and he hated Esau
point_summary: The "God loved Jacob and hated Esau" statement originates in Malachi—not Genesis—and was spoken roughly two thousand years after the events it describes, illustrating the danger of quoting Scripture without regard for its context.
